claims.claim: Proof of personhood / private unique identity
claims.label: absent

claims.claim: Network tokens with closed-loop economics (fee switches)
claims.label: confirmed

claims.claim: Company-backed tokens dressed up as decentralized (corpochains)
claims.label: confirmed

missed_changes: Heavy new emphasis on AI as a cybersecurity DEFENSE tool — 'arm the defenders,' AI red-teaming/bugfinding surfaces pre-existing bugs, 'who do you want to find them first?' (entire 5/13 hacks podcast, 4/11, 5/7 Mozilla). Not anticipated by the prediction.
Newly vocal anti-AI-doomerism — pushing back on Anthropic's 'doomsday marketing,' calling AI fear 'completely backwards,' calling for a 'moratorium on AI doomer content' (5/8, 5/13, 5/19).

Strong enthusiasm for AI-assisted software creation / vibe-coding broadly (deployed a web app via voice memo, AI code no longer 'slop,' AI-made games, the 10,000-automated-attempts loop) — wider than the prediction's crypto-specific agentic-payments framing.

Recurring pro-capitalism / value-creation philosophy as a standalone theme ('nothing more decentralized than capitalism,' AOC/Glazer dialogue, 4/7, 5/7, 5/9) — outside the prediction's sector framing.
a16z Crypto Fund 5 ($2.2B) launch as a concrete risk-on action, and the fund essay newly elevating prediction markets and onchain lending as proven sectors — neither broken out in the prediction.
notes: No contradictions; every claim is either confirmed or simply unaddressed. Strongest confirmations: network-token closed-loop economics (restated almost verbatim multiple times — 4/23 'print-and-spend... decouple fees and spend,' 4/27, 5/14), stablecoins & payments and AI-x-crypto agentic payments (both explicit in the 5/5 Fund 5 essay), operational security for vaults/bridges (5/13 podcast: time delays, multisig, single-points-of-failure, plus the 4/8 rate-limit-griefing reply continuing his 3/25 thread), and risk-on (Fund 5 + 'most encouraging in years'). Corpochains marked confirmed at the stance level: the 5/13 podcast champions exactly his 'decentralization = no single point of control; hidden/inadvertent centralization is the real danger' view, though applied to hack post-mortems rather than company-backed tokens specifically. Memecoins reaffirmed 5/2 ('uninteresting at best and damaging'). Leverage/perps left absent — the essay's positive mention of perps 'for price discovery' is co-authored and about utility, not a reversal of his leverage-risk caution, which he never revisits. ETH and UNI marked absent as specific tokens: he never names them, though the underlying buy-and-burn-plus-mint mechanism they exemplify is heavily reaffirmed (so the network-tokens SECTOR confirms while the TOKEN picks don't). Privacy, zkVMs/Jolt, proof-of-personhood and onchain games went unaddressed — the window was dominated by AI-security and AI-building commentary. Weak adjacent signals noted but insufficient to confirm: 5/6 thank-you to Alex Blania (World) for proof-of-personhood, and 6/5 praise of Zcash devs (a privacy project, but the remark is about security-disclosure handling, not the privacy thesis).
